steps.title: Identify the independent clauses being joined
steps.content: Left side of the blank: This designation is [word] forms an independent clause (subject + verb + complement). Right side: Proto-Cariban is only postulated, its characteristics being inferred from the linguistic properties of its twenty-nine daughters is also an independent clause with an additional participial phrase. Two independent clauses must be connected here.

steps.title: Apply the conjunctive adverb rule
steps.content: The word however is a conjunctive adverb that shows contrast. The SAT standard for joining two independent clauses with a conjunctive adverb is: independent clause + semicolon + however + comma + independent clause (IC; however, IC). This pattern appears in choice B: This designation is hypothetical; however, Proto-Cariban is only postulated... The semicolon separates the independent clauses while the comma properly punctuates the transition word. Choices A, C, and D violate this rule—A creates two separate sentences with a period, C places a colon before a transition word (non-standard), and D creates a comma splice by relying only on commas to separate independent clauses. That matches choice B — B is correct.
